query: strength of collagen and key components involved

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Connective Tissue -- Cellular Level
retrieved_docs.content: Fibers are another main component of connective tissue found in the extracellular matrix. Three basic fiber types are present in all connective tissue. The amount of each type generally reflects the function and classification of the particular tissue. The 3 fiber types are: Collagenous fibers: large, strong fibers, mostly commonly Type I collagen, that provide high tensile strength to the extracellular matrix, found in dense and loose connective tissue. [1] Reticular fibers: delicate, thin fibers composed of Type III collagen that cross-link to form a supporting meshwork in the reticular lamina of the basement membrane found in soft tissues such as the liver, bone marrow, spleen, and lymph nodes. [4] Elastic fibers: thin, branching fibers made of elastin that provide stretch and recoil to the extracellular matrix, found in tissues such as the aorta, lung, skin, and vocal cords. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Collagen Synthesis -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Collagen is protein molecules made up of amino acids. It provides structural support to the extracellular space of connective tissues. Due to its rigidity and resistance to stretching, it is the perfect matrix for skin, tendons, bones, and ligaments.

retrieved_docs.title: Collagen Cross Linking for Keratoconus -- Anatomy and Physiology
retrieved_docs.content: The cornea comprises distinct, densely placed, multi-scale structures with fibers of distinct patter arranged in various layers. Each layer is composed of distinct components having variations in collagen density, elastin, fibronectin, laminin, and proteoglycan. [6] These components give rise to different mechanical strengths due to various mechanical properties. The cornea is composed of 6 layers which are 550 um thick. A healthy cornea can transmit approximately 80% of light in the visible spectrum. [7] The cornea is densely packed with proteins called collagen. The collagen in the cornea resides for approximately 2-3 years. The corneal transparency is maintained by the regular packing of proteins with a proteoglycan-rich matrix, allowing the light to pass unimpeded. [8] Collagen crosslinking help in the formation of bonds between the polymeric chains of proteins. In biological tissue like cornea, these bonds strengthen the tissue, making them more resistant to mechanical degradation and deformation. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Connective Tissue -- Cellular Level
retrieved_docs.content: To further understand the physiology of connective tissue, it is essential to note the different types of collagen, which are generally composed of fibers found in the extracellular matrix. Collagen is the most abundant protein in the human body and has 28 types. [6] The 4 most common types are: Type I: the most common type; flexible, strong, provides resistance to force, tension, and stretch; found in all connective tissue, notably scar tissue, tendons, ligaments, bone, cornea, skin, and dentin. Type II: provides resistance to pressure, found in articular and hyaline cartilage of joints and intervertebral discs. Type III: provides a flexible meshwork for cellular support, the main component of reticular fibers, often found in organs such as skin and blood vessels. Also abundant during the early stages of wound healing and plays a role in granulation tissue formation. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Bone -- Structure
retrieved_docs.content: As a specialized connective tissue, bone is comprised of mainly type 1 collagen fibers and inorganic salts. Type 1 collagen is a non-calcified matrix designed to resist the tension experienced by the weight-bearing functions of bone. It forms from osteoblasts, the main cellular component of bone.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Connective Tissue -- Structure and Function -- Connective Tissue Proper
retrieved_docs.content: Collagen fibers form approximately 20-25% of the protein content of humans. They are nonelastic and have variable bundle thicknesses. They are made up of closely packed thin collagen fibrils that run a wavy course in tissues. These parallel fibrils form bundles with flexible proteoglycans to offer an essential mechanical property. They offer flexible but powerful resistance to pulling force. Specifically, collagen runs in a parallel course in loose connective tissue and then joins to form a larger bundle. They split from each other and join back together at varying locations, creating a three-dimensional meshwork. Dense connective tissue, such as ligaments and tendons, is composed mainly of densely packed collagen fibers. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Collagen Synthesis -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: Collagen is the most abundant protein in the human body. Therefore, it can be divided into many types. The most common types of collagen are types I through V each serving different functions.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Collagen Synthesis -- Fundamentals
retrieved_docs.content: Collagen is composed of 3 chains. The chains are wound together to form a triple helix. Since glycine is the smallest of all the amino acids, it allows the chain to form a tight configuration, and and it can withstand stress.

retrieved_docs.title: Collagen Cross Linking for Keratoconus -- Anatomy and Physiology
retrieved_docs.content: The different macromolecules have varied subunits that point to other mechanical functions or different evolutionary developmental origins. Another group of molecules is glycosaminoglycans (GAG) which attract and retain water, absorb the shock, and impact tissue viscosity. [14] Proteoglycans are a group of proteins in which GAG is attached to one or more core proteins, retaining a similar function to other GAGs. Based on this knowledge, hypotheses about each corneal later can be made. The tear film is a liquid interface with no major polymeric components. Hence it is thought to have little direct mechanical impact on the cornea. The epithelial and endothelial cell layers also lack any regular protein framework; therefore, they contribute little mechanical strength to the cornea. [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Connective Tissue -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Ascorbic acid (Vitamin C) is essential for activating the enzyme prolyl hydroxylase, which promotes the hydroxylation step in forming the hydroxyproline, an integral constituent of collagen. Without ascorbic acid, the collagen fibers in virtually all body tissues are defective and weak. Therefore, this vitamin is important for the growth and strength of the fibers in subcutaneous tissue, cartilage, bone, and teeth. Particularly deficiency of the ascorbic acid for about 20-30 weeks can cause scurvy. One of the most important effects of scurvy is the failure of wounds to heal. [20]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Bone -- Function -- Bone Extracellular Matrix
retrieved_docs.content: The bone extracellular matrix makes up 90% of the overall bone volume and consists of inorganic (mineral) and organic matrices. Inorganic bone matrix accounts for 99% of the body's storage of calcium, 85% of the phosphorus, and 40% to 60% of the magnesium and sodium. It is mainly in the form of hydroxyapatite [Ca10(PO4)6(OH)2] to provide the bone its strength, stiffness, and resistance to compressive forces. Organic bone matrix is secreted by osteoblasts and is predominantly type I collagen; this also contains glycoproteins, growth factors, and proteoglycans. Growth factors (such as osteocalcin, osteonectin, and bone sialoprotein) play important roles in osteoid formation, mineralization, and bone remodeling. The organic matrix gives bone its form and provides resistance to tensile forces. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Connective Tissue -- Cellular Level -- Collagen Synthesis and Structure
retrieved_docs.content: Next, the hydroxylated lysine of the alpha chain undergoes glycosylation with the addition of a carbohydrate. After glycosylation, the formation of disulfide bonds and hydrogen bonds between 3 different alpha chains forms a triple helix. The resultant structure is now procollagen. Defective triple helix formation leads to osteogenesis imperfecta and impaired bone matrix synthesis. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Connective Tissue -- Cellular Level -- Collagen Synthesis and Structure
retrieved_docs.content: Next, tropocollagen is reinforced with many adjacent tropocollagen molecules by covalent cross-linking of hydroxylated lysine residues, resulting in collagen fibrils. The cross-linking of hydroxylysine residues is made possible through a copper-dependent lysyl oxidase enzyme. Finally, several collagen fibrils accumulate to form a thick bundle; the end product is collagen fibers. [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Connective Tissue -- Cellular Level -- Collagen Synthesis and Structure
retrieved_docs.content: Procollagen then shuttles to the extracellular space through exocytosis. Once in the extracellular space, it is cleaved at the C-terminus and N-terminus, forming what is now called tropocollagen. As a result of the cleavage, tropocollagen is insoluble in water.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Collagen Synthesis -- Clinical Significance -- Scurvy
retrieved_docs.content: OI affects 1 in 15,000 people, and diagnosis is made clinically and can be confirmed by DNA or collagen testing. Prognosis depends on the type of OI. There is no cure and treatment is supportive and based on the prevention of fractures. Bisphosphonates, surgery, and physiotherapy have been shown to help. Ehlers-Danlos Syndrome (EDS )

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 1918
retrieved_docs.content: Replacement preparation is a medical formulation that incorporates several specific components, including kalcium‑glycerolfosfát, dibasic potassium phosphate, and sodium hyaluronate. Kalcium‑glycerolfosfát contributes calcium and phosphate ions that can support mineral balance, while dibasic potassium phosphate provides additional phosphate and potassium ions to help maintain physiological pH and electrolyte homeostasis. Sodium hyaluronate, a high‑molecular‑weight glycosaminoglycan, offers viscoelastic properties that are valuable for joint lubrication and tissue repair. Together, these ingredients define the composition of the replacement preparation, highlighting its role in delivering essential minerals and lubricating agents for therapeutic use.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2710
retrieved_docs.content: Connective tissue protein is linked to both kyselina hyaluronová and kolagen, indicating that it is considered a subclass or component of these two major extracellular‑matrix substances. The ReFit bone substitute is mapped to kyselina hyaluronová and also to kolagen, showing that the product incorporates or is designed to emulate these key connective‑tissue constituents. Together, these relationships underscore the importance of hyaluronic acid (kyselina hyaluronová) and collagen (kolagen) as foundational elements of connective tissue proteins and as integral components in the formulation of bone‑substitute materials, which can enhance biocompatibility and support bone regeneration in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 1827
retrieved_docs.content: Replacement preparation is a therapeutic formulation that incorporates kalcium-glycerolfosfát and sodium hyaluronate as its constituent components. Kalcium-glycerolfosfát provides a calcium‑phosphate source that can support mineralization processes, while sodium hyaluronate contributes viscoelastic and lubricating properties useful for tissue repair and joint function. By combining these two agents, the replacement preparation is designed to enhance clinical outcomes in procedures that require both structural support and improved biomechanical performance.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1463
retrieved_docs.content: Hyaluronic acid (kyselina hyaluronová) and collagen (kolagen) are highlighted as primary components of connective tissue ultrastructure, and both are listed as allowed qualifiers for describing ultrastructural features. Protein hydrolysates (proteinové hydrolyzáty) are similarly recognized as an allowed qualifier for ultrastructure, emphasizing their relevance in detailed tissue morphology. Connective tissue proteins are conceptually linked to both hyaluronic acid and collagen, reflecting their shared role in the extracellular matrix and structural integrity of connective tissues. The ReFit bone substitute is specifically mapped to hyaluronic acid and collagen, indicating that this biomaterial incorporates these two key extracellular matrix constituents to mimic natural bone tissue architecture. Together, these relationships underscore the importance of hyaluronic acid, collagen, and protein hydrolysates in defining tissue ultrastructure and in the design of biomimetic bone substitutes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 462
retrieved_docs.content: Other bone disease and musculoskeletal deformities are closely linked to inflammation (الالْتِهاب) and to bone resorption (resorpce kosti). These associations indicate that inflammatory processes and the loss of bone tissue are central mechanisms underlying a range of skeletal disorders and deformities. Recognizing the connection between inflammation and bone resorption can guide diagnostic evaluation and therapeutic strategies aimed at preserving bone integrity and preventing further musculoskeletal complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 2 -- Community 910
retrieved_docs.content: The community describes a network in which the metabolic domain (metabolismus) is qualified by a variety of biological substances—including amylases, blood‑coagulation factors (faktory krevní koagulace), karaya gum (guma karaya), immunostimulants, bone morphogenetic proteins (kostní morfogenetické proteiny), and hyaluronic acid (kyselina hyaluronová). Metabolismus is further linked to the therapeutic agents omalizumab and thyroglobulin, indicating metabolic considerations for these drugs. Immunostimulants are classified as a type of hyaluronic acid, and the compound acetylmuramyl‑alanyl‑isoglutamin is identified as an immunostimulant. Hyaluronic acid and collagen (kolagen) are each characterized by ultrastructural (ultrastruktura) attributes, a property also ascribed to karaya gum, amylases, thyroglobulin, and blood‑coagulation factors. Connective‑tissue protein is defined as having hyaluronic acid and collagen as parent components, reflecting its composition from these extracellular‑matrix molecules. The ReFit bone substitute is mapped to both hyaluronic acid and collagen, indicating incorporation of these substances in the biomaterial. Bone morphogenetic proteins are highlighted as principal regulators of bone tissue ultrastructure, and this relationship is recognized as an allowed qualifier. The therapeutic class “drugs affecting bone structure and mineralization” is closely associated with bone morphogenetic proteins, underscoring the targeting of BMP pathways in treatments for bone remodeling disorders. Omalizumab, while primarily an anti‑IgE monoclonal antibody, is also linked to ultrastructure, suggesting potential effects on tissue architecture. Collectively, these interconnections illustrate how metabolic qualifiers, extracellular‑matrix components, immunostimulatory agents, bone‑regulating proteins, and therapeutic biomaterials interact to shape tissue ultrastructure and inform clinical strategies for bone‑related, immunologic, and metabolic conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1296
retrieved_docs.content: Connective tissue protein is described as having both kolagen (collagen) and kyselina hyaluronová (hyaluronic acid) as parent components, indicating that these macromolecules contribute to its structure or classification. In this representation, kyselina hyaluronová is further placed under the broader category of proteiny (proteins), reflecting its inclusion within protein‑related pathways. The ReFit bone substitute is mapped to both kyselina hyaluronová and kolagen, suggesting that the product incorporates these biomaterials to emulate the natural extracellular matrix of bone. Together, these relationships underscore the interconnected roles of kolagen, kyselina hyaluronová, and the protein family in connective tissue composition and in the design of bone‑substituting materials.
